title: Fix `podman inspect` to correctly handle log_size_max
body:
When generating Conmon's command line, we read containers.conf to get log_size_max and used it if the container didn't override it. However, `podman inspect` only reads from the container's own config, and ignores containers.conf. Unify the way we determine maximum log size with a single function and use it for both inspect and containers.conf, and add a test for this behavior.

Fixes https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RHEL-96776

```release-note
Fixed a bug where `podman inspect` did not correctly display log size for containers when `log_size_max` was set in containers.conf.
```

comments.body:
```
# #|     FAIL: podman inspect --format {{ .HostConfig.LogConfig.Size }} c_t48-ezu0uhig
         # #| expected: '400mb'
         # #|   actual: '400MB'
```

Very curious that we're getting uppercase `MB` in CI, because I get lowercase running the same commands locally.

> ```
> # #|     FAIL: podman inspect --format {{ .HostConfig.LogConfig.Size }} c_t48-ezu0uhig
>          # #| expected: '400mb'
>          # #|   actual: '400MB'
> ```
> 
> Very curious that we're getting uppercase `MB` in CI, because I get lowercase running the same commands locally.

looking at the docker go-units code I see
`decimapAbbrs = []string{"B", "kB", "MB", "GB", "TB", "PB", "EB", "ZB", "YB"}` as units for the units.HumanSize() call so it does seem odd that kB is small while MB is not.
But the code should be deterministic so I don't understand why you local result would be any different from CI.

I was considering setting LogSize during container creation if it wasn't explicitly overridden by the user, but that is a behavior change - as it is right now, people can change these limits on the fly by altering the config file. It's also a bit of an edge case but it could be considered a breaking change that configuration file changes will now be ignored on container restart and require actual recreation to take effect.
(If we want strict accuracy, I suppose I could cache the value used in container state whenever we start the container, but that seems excessive?)

> I was considering setting LogSize during container creation if it wasn't explicitly overridden by the user, but that is a behavior change - as it is right now, people can change these limits on the fly by altering the config file. It's also a bit of an edge case but it could be considered a breaking change that configuration file changes will now be ignored on container restart and require actual recreation to take effect.

Sure I was not trying to suggest that we commit it into the container config. The proper fix would be to write it into the container state.
If I do add caching, I would want to rip it out in Podman 6 and move to the simpler "set once at create time" behavior - which matches how we do almost all our other config fields.

> how we do almost all our other config fields.

I am not to sure what the percentage is without proper analysis. But I guess that is a another topic for our containers.conf rewrite stuff if these settings should persist in the container config or not. With stuff like toolbox we often get burned by committing values in the config with now way to alter them later.

In any case if you want to you don't want the state cache I am ok to merge the PR like this as well, it doesn't fix the problem fully but it does fix the main issue with the config value being ignored.
I think we should merge as-is with a goal of fixing this by properly handling in container config in 6.0
